title: Mental Health at Work
summary: Two colleagues discuss the challenges of workplace stress and mental health, sharing personal experiences and practical strategies.

body.speaker: Colleague1
body.text: Hey, Emma. You got a minute? I wanted to check in. You've seemed a bit off this past week.

body.speaker: Colleague2
body.text: Oh, is it that obvious? I didn't want to make it anyone else's problem. I've just been really struggling with the workload lately.

body.speaker: Colleague1
body.text: You don't have to carry it on your own. What's been going on?

body.speaker: Colleague2
body.text: Honestly, I think it's a combination of things. The project deadlines have been stacking up, and I feel like I can never switch off. Even at home, I'm still thinking about work.

body.speaker: Colleague1
body.text: I know that feeling well. Last year I went through something similar, and I wish I had spoken to someone earlier instead of bottling it all up.

body.speaker: Colleague2
body.text: Did it get quite bad for you?

body.speaker: Colleague1
body.text: It did, yeah. If I hadn't finally talked to my manager about it, I think I would have burned out completely. She was surprisingly supportive, actually.

body.speaker: Colleague2
body.text: That's reassuring to hear. I've been worried about how it would look if I raised it with management. Like I can't handle the pressure.

body.speaker: Colleague1
body.text: I completely understand that fear. But it's been reported that most managers would rather have that conversation early than deal with the consequences of someone reaching their limit.

body.speaker: Colleague2
body.text: I hadn't thought about it from their perspective. Do you think our manager would be receptive?

body.speaker: Colleague1
body.text: Based on my experience, yes. She's made it clear that mental health is taken seriously on this team. If you approached her with specific examples rather than a vague sense of being overwhelmed, she would be more able to help.

body.speaker: Colleague2
body.text: That's actually a really practical tip. I tend to say I'm fine when I'm not, because I don't want to seem weak.

body.speaker: Colleague1
body.text: You and everyone else. But the thing is, being honest about your limits takes more courage than pretending you're coping.

body.speaker: Colleague2
body.text: I've also been trying some things on my own — setting a cut-off time for emails and taking proper lunch breaks. But some days it feels like it's not enough.

body.speaker: Colleague1
body.text: Those are great strategies, but they're harder to maintain when the environment around you doesn't support them. That's exactly why it's worth having the conversation with management.

body.speaker: Colleague2
body.text: You're right. I think I've been so focused on appearing competent that I've neglected my own needs. I'll try to book some time with her this week.

body.speaker: Colleague1
body.text: Good. And in the meantime, don't hesitate to come to me. Sometimes just talking about it makes things feel more manageable.

body.speaker: Colleague2
body.text: Thanks, Brian. I really appreciate it. It actually helps a lot just knowing someone noticed.

questions.prompt: Why does Brian initiate the conversation?
options: A. He needs Emma's help with a project
B. He noticed Emma seemed off this past week
C. He wants to complain about the workload himself
D. He was asked to check on her by their manager
questions.explanation: Brian says: 'You've seemed a bit off this past week' — he noticed a change and wanted to check in.
questions.hint: Listen to why Brian approaches Emma at the start.

questions.prompt: What is Emma primarily struggling with?
options: C. The workload and an inability to switch off
questions.explanation: Emma says: 'The project deadlines have been stacking up, and I feel like I can never switch off.'

questions.prompt: What does Brian wish he had done differently when he went through something similar?
options: A. Asked for a reduced workload sooner
C. Spoken to someone earlier instead of bottling it up
questions.explanation: Brian says: 'I wish I had spoken to someone earlier instead of bottling it all up.'
questions.hint: He uses 'I wish I had...' to express regret about the past.

questions.prompt: What third conditional does Brian use to describe his own experience?
options: A. If I don't talk to my manager, I will burn out
B. If I hadn't talked to my manager, I would have burned out completely
C. If I talked to my manager, things would be better
D. If I had known earlier, I would not have struggled
questions.explanation: Brian says: 'If I hadn't finally talked to my manager about it, I think I would have burned out completely.'
questions.hint: He is describing a past situation with a hypothetical past outcome.

questions.prompt: What is Emma afraid will happen if she raises her mental health concerns with management?
options: A. She will lose her job
B. Her colleagues will find out
C. She will look as though she cannot handle pressure
D. She will be moved to a different team
questions.explanation: Emma says: 'I've been worried about how it would look if I raised it with management. Like I can't handle the pressure.'
questions.hint: Emma is concerned about how she will appear professionally.

questions.prompt: What does Brian say has been reported about managers?
options: A. They prefer to avoid these conversations
B. Most would rather have the conversation early than deal with the consequences later
C. They are trained to detect burnout
D. They are required by law to discuss mental health
questions.explanation: Brian says: 'it's been reported that most managers would rather have that conversation early than deal with the consequences of someone reaching their limit.'
questions.hint: Brian uses the passive 'it's been reported' to cite general knowledge.

questions.prompt: What second conditional advice does Brian give Emma about approaching their manager?
options: A. If you approached her with data, she would understand
B. If you approach her with examples, she will help
C. If you approached her with specific examples, she would be more able to help
D. If you had approached her earlier, she would have helped
questions.explanation: Brian uses a second conditional: 'If you approached her with specific examples rather than a vague sense of being overwhelmed, she would be more able to help.'
questions.hint: Brian gives hypothetical advice about how Emma could approach the conversation.

questions.prompt: What does Brian say mental health is on their team, according to the manager's signals?
questions.explanation: Brian says: 'She's made it clear that mental health is taken seriously on this team.'
questions.hint: He uses a passive form to describe the team's stance.

questions.prompt: What habit does Emma describe as part of her self-care routine?
options: C. Setting a cut-off time for emails and taking proper lunch breaks
D. Working from home on Fridays
questions.explanation: Emma says she has been 'setting a cut-off time for emails and taking proper lunch breaks.'
questions.hint: Emma describes two practical boundaries she has tried to set.

questions.prompt: According to Brian, why are individual coping strategies harder to maintain?
options: A. They require too much discipline
B. They conflict with company policy
C. They are harder to maintain when the environment does not support them
D. They are seen as a sign of weakness
questions.explanation: Brian says: 'they're harder to maintain when the environment around you doesn't support them.'
questions.hint: Brian points to a structural issue, not a personal failing.

questions.prompt: What does Emma say she has neglected because of her focus on appearing competent?
questions.explanation: Emma says: 'I've been so focused on appearing competent that I've neglected my own needs.'
questions.hint: Emma makes a self-aware confession near the end of the conversation.

questions.prompt: What does Brian say being honest about your limits requires?
questions.explanation: Brian says: 'being honest about your limits takes more courage than pretending you're coping.'
questions.hint: Brian reframes honesty as a strength, not a weakness.

questions.prompt: What action does Emma decide to take at the end of the conversation?
options: A. Work from home for the rest of the week
B. Ask for a formal review of her workload
C. Book time with their manager this week
D. Take a few days off
questions.explanation: Emma says: 'I'll try to book some time with her this week.'
questions.hint: Emma commits to a specific, practical next step.

questions.prompt: What does Emma say helps at the very end of the conversation?
options: A. Having a clear plan of action
D. Feeling less alone at work
questions.explanation: Emma says: 'It actually helps a lot just knowing someone noticed.'
questions.hint: Emma's final words reveal what made the biggest difference.

questions.prompt: What is the overall message of the dialogue about mental health in the workplace?
options: A. Mental health problems are the responsibility of HR
B. Talking openly about struggles and seeking support are signs of strength
C. Individual coping strategies are sufficient to manage stress
D. Managers are usually unsympathetic to mental health concerns
questions.explanation: Throughout the dialogue, Brian encourages Emma to speak up and frames honesty about struggles as courageous rather than weak.
questions.hint: Consider the overall values Brian expresses throughout the conversation.

cloze.template: Brian started the conversation because he had noticed that Emma had seemed a bit {{b1}} that week. Emma admitted she had been {{b2}} with the workload and that she could never {{b3}} off, even at home. Brian shared that he had gone through something {{b4}} the previous year and {{b5}} he had spoken to someone earlier instead of bottling it all up. He told Emma that if he hadn't {{b6}} talked to his manager, he would have {{b7}} out completely. Brian also pointed out that it had been {{b8}} that most managers would rather have the conversation {{b9}} than deal with the consequences later. He reassured Emma that mental health was {{b10}} seriously on their team. He suggested that if Emma {{b11}} her manager with specific examples, the manager would be more able to help. Emma had been worried that raising the issue would make her appear unable to {{b12}} pressure. Brian reminded her that being {{b13}} about one's limits takes more {{b14}} than pretending to cope. Emma had already tried setting a {{b15}} time for emails and taking proper {{b16}} breaks, but Brian noted these strategies were harder to {{b17}} without a supportive environment. In the end, Emma decided to book some time with her manager that {{b18}}.

blanks.explanation: Brian says Emma had 'seemed a bit off this past week'.
Emma says: 'I've just been really struggling with the workload'.
Emma says: 'I feel like I can never switch off'.
Brian says: 'I went through something similar'.
Brian says: 'I wish I had spoken to someone earlier' — expressing regret about the past.
Brian says: 'If I hadn't finally talked to my manager...' — third conditional.
Brian says: 'I would have burned out completely' — third conditional result.
Brian says: 'it's been reported that most managers would rather...' — passive construction.
Brian says managers 'would rather have that conversation early'.
Brian says: 'mental health is taken seriously on this team' — passive voice.
Second conditional: 'If you approached her with specific examples...'.
Emma worries she will look like she 'can't handle the pressure'.
Brian says: 'being honest about your limits takes more courage'.
Brian says honesty 'takes more courage than pretending you're coping'.
Emma mentions 'setting a cut-off time for emails'.
Emma says she has been 'taking proper lunch breaks'.
Brian says individual strategies are 'harder to maintain when the environment doesn't support them'.
Emma says: 'I'll try to book some time with her this week'.

annotations.meaningVi: hỏi thăm / kiểm tra xem ai đó có ổn không
annotations.exampleEn: I just wanted to check in and see how you were doing after the difficult week.

annotations.meaningVi: trông có vẻ không ổn / không bình thường
annotations.exampleEn: She seemed a bit off at the meeting — I wondered if something was wrong.

annotations.meaningVi: khối lượng công việc
annotations.exampleEn: His workload has increased significantly since two colleagues resigned.

annotations.meaningVi: chồng chất / tích lũy
annotations.exampleEn: The emails kept stacking up while she was on leave.

annotations.meaningVi: ngừng suy nghĩ về công việc / tắt trạng thái làm việc
annotations.exampleEn: He found it impossible to switch off after a stressful day at the office.

annotations.meaningVi: kìm nén cảm xúc / giữ trong lòng
annotations.exampleEn: Rather than bottling up her frustration, she chose to speak to her manager.

annotations.meaningVi: kiệt sức / bị kiệt quệ vì làm việc quá sức
annotations.exampleEn: He burned out after working eighty-hour weeks for six months.

annotations.meaningVi: cởi mở tiếp nhận / sẵn sàng lắng nghe
annotations.exampleEn: The manager was receptive to feedback about the team's working conditions.

annotations.meaningVi: bị quá tải / choáng ngợp
annotations.exampleEn: She felt completely overwhelmed by the number of tasks on her list.

annotations.meaningVi: lời khuyên thực tế / mẹo hữu ích
annotations.exampleEn: He shared some practical tips for managing deadlines more effectively.

annotations.meaningVi: thời gian dừng / giới hạn thời gian
annotations.exampleEn: She set a cut-off time of seven in the evening for checking work messages.

annotations.meaningVi: duy trì
annotations.exampleEn: It's difficult to maintain healthy habits when the workplace culture doesn't support them.

annotations.meaningVi: tỏ ra có năng lực
annotations.exampleEn: She was so focused on appearing competent that she never asked for help.

annotations.meaningVi: bỏ bê nhu cầu của bản thân
annotations.exampleEn: High achievers often neglect their own needs in pursuit of professional goals.

annotations.meaningVi: có thể kiểm soát được / đỡ nặng nề hơn
annotations.exampleEn: Talking about the problem made it feel more manageable.

annotations.meaningVi: giả vờ ổn / vờ như mình vẫn chịu đựng được
annotations.exampleEn: He had been pretending to cope for months before finally seeking help.

annotations.meaningVi: đến giới hạn chịu đựng
annotations.exampleEn: Warning signs that someone is reaching their limit include irritability and poor sleep.

annotations.meaningVi: nêu lên / đặt vấn đề
annotations.exampleEn: She was nervous about raising the issue with her supervisor.

annotations.meaningVi: trấn an / đem lại sự yên tâm
annotations.exampleEn: It was reassuring to hear that others had faced the same challenge.

annotations.meaningVi: đừng ngần ngại / hãy cứ mạnh dạn
annotations.exampleEn: Don't hesitate to contact me if you need any further assistance.

grammarNotes.title: Third Conditional — Expressing Regret and Hypothetical Outcomes
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong bài nghe này, câu điều kiện loại 3 được dùng để diễn đạt hối tiếc và kết quả giả định trong quá khứ. Ví dụ: 'If I hadn't finally talked to my manager, I think I would have burned out completely.' Cấu trúc: If + hadn't + V3, would/might have + V3. Lưu ý: 'I wish I had spoken to someone earlier' cũng là cách diễn đạt hối tiếc về quá khứ, tương đương về nghĩa nhưng khác về hình thức so với câu điều kiện loại 3.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The third conditional appears twice in this dialogue to express regret about past decisions and their hypothetical outcomes. Key example: 'If I hadn't finally talked to my manager about it, I think I would have burned out completely.' Structure: If + past perfect (negative), would have + past participle. Also note: 'I wish I had spoken to someone earlier' is a related regret structure using 'wish + past perfect', which expresses the same meaning as a third conditional but in a different grammatical form: 'I wish I had [done X]' ≈ 'If I had [done X], things would have been better.'

grammarNotes.title: Second Conditional for Hypothetical Advice
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu điều kiện loại 2 được dùng để đưa ra lời khuyên về tình huống giả định trong hiện tại hoặc tương lai. Ví dụ: 'If you approached her with specific examples rather than a vague sense of being overwhelmed, she would be more able to help.' Cấu trúc: If + V2 (past simple), would + V infinitive. Câu điều kiện loại 2 thường được dùng khi người nói muốn đề xuất hành động mà không ép buộc, lịch sự hơn so với mệnh lệnh trực tiếp.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The second conditional is used to offer gentle, hypothetical advice about a present or future course of action. Example: 'If you approached her with specific examples rather than a vague sense of being overwhelmed, she would be more able to help.' Structure: If + past simple, would + base verb. Using the second conditional for advice ('If you did X, it would help') is more polite and tentative than a direct imperative ('Do X'), which is why it is common in mentoring, coaching, and peer-support conversations.

translationVi: Brian bắt đầu cuộc trò chuyện vì anh nhận thấy Emma có vẻ không ổn trong tuần qua. Emma thú nhận rằng cô đang gặp khó khăn với khối lượng công việc và không thể ngừng nghĩ về công việc ngay cả khi ở nhà. Brian chia sẻ rằng anh đã trải qua điều tương tự vào năm ngoái và ước gì mình đã nói chuyện với ai đó sớm hơn thay vì kìm nén mọi thứ. Anh nói rằng nếu anh không nói chuyện với người quản lý, anh nghĩ anh sẽ kiệt sức hoàn toàn. Brian cũng chỉ ra rằng hầu hết các nhà quản lý muốn có cuộc trò chuyện đó sớm hơn là phải đối phó với hậu quả. Anh trấn an Emma rằng nếu cô tiếp cận người quản lý với các ví dụ cụ thể, người quản lý sẽ có thể giúp đỡ hơn. Emma đã lo lắng rằng việc nêu lên vấn đề sẽ khiến cô trông như không thể chịu đựng được áp lực. Brian nhắc nhở cô rằng thành thật về giới hạn của mình cần nhiều can đảm hơn là giả vờ ổn. Cuối cùng, Emma quyết định đặt lịch gặp người quản lý trong tuần đó.
criticalThinkingQuestion: Brian suggests that individual coping strategies are less effective without a supportive organisational environment. To what extent should employers be held responsible for the mental health of their employees, and what structural changes could workplaces make to genuinely reduce stress rather than simply offering wellness perks?
